Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Machine Minds

Rodney Brooks

b. 1954

Embodied robotics and intelligence without detached inner representation.

The compressed thesis is this: Brooks is a naturalistic mechanist who rejects mind–body dualism and any “soul” understood as an extra causal substance, but he is not a crude computationalist. He does not think mind is merely symbol manipulation inside a Cartesian head. For Brooks, mind is embodied, situated, behaviorally distributed, affectively regulated, environmentally coupled, and emergent. Consciousness is not ruled out by machinehood, because humans themselves are machines in his broad biomolecular sense; but consciousness remains scientifically underdefined, and the eventual recognition of robot consciousness may depend partly on social, relational, and ethical practices of attribution. The theological significance of Brooks’s work is therefore not a side issue. It presses exactly where his mechanistic ontology meets human practices of love, recognition, dignity, and personhood.

1. Brooks’s starting point: against the Cartesian picture of mind

Brooks’s mature position is best understood as a rejection of the classical artificial-intelligence picture that treated intelligence as internal symbolic reasoning: sense the world, construct representations, plan, then act. In “Elephants Don’t Play Chess,” he argues that the traditional “symbol system” approach rests on a flawed understanding of intelligence, because symbols are not intrinsically grounded; they derive their meaning only through their relation to lived, bodily, worldly engagement. He proposes instead a “physical grounding” approach: intelligence begins from an embodied system acting in the world, not from a disembodied reasoning engine manipulating representations.

This is also the center of “Intelligence without Representation.” Brooks says classical AI “foundered” on the problem of representation. Once a system is embedded in real-world perception and action, the need for large internal representations diminishes: the world itself can serve as its own model, because a creature can look, touch, move, and update rather than carry around an exhaustive internal substitute for reality.

This anti-Cartesianism has several layers.

First, Brooks rejects the idea of a central inner theater where the mind collects data, constructs a world-model, and then issues commands. His behavior-based robots are built from multiple interacting competence layers, each connected relatively directly to perception and action. There is no privileged homunculus. The appearance of unified intelligence arises from distributed behavioral dynamics. Brooks writes that an outside observer may impute central representation and control, but the creature itself consists of competing and cooperating behaviors, with coherent action emerging at the level of the whole organism or robot.

Second, Brooks rejects the idea that “reasoning” is the foundation of intelligence. In “Intelligence Without Reason,” he emphasizes situatedness and embodiment: a system’s intelligence is not merely in its internal computation, but in the way perception, body, action, and environment form a coupled dynamical system. He explicitly denies that intelligence requires a homunculus, central reasoning system, manipulable symbolic representations, or a detached planning module.

Third, Brooks reorders the hierarchy of cognition. Classical AI begins with reasoning, language, planning, and abstract problem-solving. Brooks begins with animal competence: moving, avoiding obstacles, approaching, withdrawing, tracking, responding, maintaining bodily integrity, interacting socially. Intelligence is not first a contemplative faculty. It is first a worldly capacity to cope.

In this sense Brooks belongs within the broader embodied-cognition tradition, which challenges the idea that cognition is best modeled as computation over internal symbols. Contemporary embodied cognition emphasizes real-time organism–environment interaction, the role of sensorimotor capacities, and the possibility that the brain is not the sole seat of cognition in the narrow internalist sense.

The important point is that Brooks is not simply saying “robots can be intelligent too.” He is saying that the philosophical picture of intelligence inherited by much of AI was wrong. Intelligence is not essentially the manipulation of explicit mental contents. It is a pattern of embodied activity.

2. Ontology: humans are machines, but not in the trivial computer sense

Brooks’s most famous and most provocative metaphysical claim is that human beings are machines. In Flesh and Machines, he states that molecular biology leaves no need for a mind–body dualism: mind is a product of brain operation, and the brain is made of biomolecules. He extends this to the whole body: every component of the human organism operates according to physics and chemistry. Hence, “we are machines,” as are spouses, children, and dogs.

This is a real anti-dualist position. Brooks rejects the soul as a special explanatory ingredient if “soul” means an immaterial substance that performs causal work beyond biology, chemistry, and physics. He is also anti-vitalist: there is no extra life-force, no élan vital, no ghostly agency hidden behind the organism.

But Brooks’s machine thesis must not be misread. He is not saying that humans are like desktop computers, nor that cognition is reducible to conventional software execution. In “The Relationship Between Matter and Life,” he says the current scientific picture treats living things as machines whose components are biomolecules, but he also stresses that artificial intelligence and artificial life have not yet produced artifacts that are confused with genuinely living organisms for more than an instant. Present systems lack the “presence” and awareness even of simple animals.

This matters because Brooks’s mechanistic materialism is not the same as computational reductionism. He is mechanistic about ontology, but pluralistic and humble about organization. Living systems obey physical law, yet we may lack the right mathematics to understand how life, cognition, perception, learning, and consciousness are organized. In the same essay, Brooks considers hypotheses that would introduce “new stuff,” including Penrose-style quantum accounts, Chalmers-style fundamental consciousness properties, or older ideas like soul and élan vital. Brooks’s preferred alternative is not new substance or new physics, but “new mathematics”: undiscovered principles for understanding the organization of living matter and cognition under ordinary physical law.

So the Brooksian ontology is:

Matter is sufficient, but our present models of matter-in-organization may be insufficient.

That is a significant philosophical position. It avoids both dualism and premature reduction. It says that consciousness, mind, and life do not require nonphysical substance; but it also says that existing computational models may be shallow caricatures of living organization.

3. The architecture of mind: distributed, embodied, and emergent

Brooks’s technical robotics work supplies the architecture behind his philosophy of mind. His robots are not designed as central reasoners. They are layered, reactive, behavior-producing systems. Simple behavioral competences are combined into more complex wholes. Intelligence is built “bottom up,” beginning with capacities for situated coping.

In “Elephants Don’t Play Chess,” he argues that instead of decomposing intelligence into high-level cognitive modules such as perception, planning, and symbolic reasoning, one can decompose systems into behavior-generating modules. Complex behavior emerges from the interactions among these modules and the world.

This leads to a distinctive account of mental unity. The unity of the mind is not produced by a central executive that surveys all information and issues commands. It is produced by dynamic coordination among subsystems, body, and environment. A robot or animal can behave coherently without possessing a unified symbolic representation of itself and its world. Brooks’s phrase “the world is its own best model” captures the point: rather than internally store everything, an embodied agent can continually consult the world through perception and action.

This architecture implies a strong anti-homuncularism. There is no inner person inside the person. There is no hidden spectator, no central soul-like controller, no mental executive that duplicates the whole organism at a smaller scale. What we call mind is the emergent organization of many processes whose coordination gives rise to intelligent behavior.

This also explains why Brooks’s philosophy is not behaviorism in the crude sense. He is interested in internal organization, but not in internal organization understood as symbolic propositions inside a Cartesian mental space. The relevant internals are drives, sensors, feedback loops, motor capacities, affective modulations, bodily states, and action-oriented couplings with the world.

A Spanish-language philosophical thesis from the Universidad de Chile reads Brooks in precisely this way: Brooksian creatures are dynamically coupled to their environments, pursue multiple parallel goals, and generate higher-level intelligence from perception–action processes. The thesis emphasizes that Brooks replaces the horizontal structure of classical AI, with its central processor and deliberative stage, by systems in which multiple behaviors operate without a single central regulator.

The philosophical consequence is this: mind is not a thing located in a box. Mind is organized activity.

4. Grounding: why symbols are not enough

Brooks’s critique of representation is also a critique of ungrounded symbol manipulation. In classical AI, a system may manipulate symbols according to formal rules without those symbols being meaningfully connected to the world. Brooks objects that symbols acquire meaning through physical and practical grounding. They are not self-interpreting.

In “Elephants Don’t Play Chess,” he argues that the meanings of symbols are not important to the reasoner itself in classical AI; coherence is often visible only to an observer who can ground the symbols in their own experience.

This is why embodiment is not an optional add-on for Brooks. A robot’s body is not merely the place where intelligence is housed; it is part of the grounding relation that makes intelligence possible. A system that acts, perceives, corrects, fails, learns, and maintains itself in a world has a kind of contact with reality that an abstract symbol system lacks.

Brooks later acknowledged that large language models challenge his older arguments about grounding. In a 2023 essay, he wrote that ChatGPT forced him to rethink some of his previous language-grounding commitments. He still did not think such systems understand language in the same way physically grounded humans do, but he admitted that his earlier position had become intellectually unstable in light of LLM performance.

That concession is philosophically important. It shows that Brooks’s grounding thesis is empirical and revisable, not a dogma. But his later writings still preserve the basic distinction between performance and competence. He repeatedly warns that AI observers confuse impressive task performance with deeper competence or understanding. A system may label images, play games, or produce language without possessing the rich worldly understanding humans attribute to one another.

His 2025 reflections on humanoids and embodied intelligence continue the same theme. He is skeptical that humanoid robots will soon generally perform human manual tasks, partly because human hands and bodies possess dense tactile, force, and sensorimotor capacities that current robots lack. At the same time, he recognizes that modern AI has made surprising progress through end-to-end learning, including in disembodied language systems.

So the final Brooksian grounding view is not “disembodied systems can do nothing intelligent.” It is subtler: disembodied systems may achieve striking performances, but full animal-like or human-like competence requires dense grounding in world, body, action, and social learning.

5. Emotion: not decoration, but part of cognition

Brooks’s work on Kismet and related social robots shows that his philosophy of mind is not coldly intellectualist. Emotion is not treated as a decorative interface layered on top of cognition. It is part of the cognitive economy.

In Flesh and Machines, Brooks describes Kismet and My Real Baby as machines with internal emotional systems tied to drives such as loneliness and arousal. Their perceptual and motor systems interact with emotional state: a change in “loneliness,” for instance, changes what the robot attends to and how it behaves. He explicitly connects this with neurological accounts, especially Antonio Damasio’s argument that emotion plays a deep role in rational decision-making.

This is a crucial anti-Cartesian move. Descartes and many post-Cartesian traditions privilege reason as the distinctively mental faculty and treat emotion as bodily disturbance. Brooks’s robots reverse the order. Emotional regulation is a structural feature of situated intelligence. A social creature does not first form detached beliefs and then optionally add feelings. Its attention, salience, action tendencies, and social interactions are affectively shaped from the beginning.

Brooks is also unusually careful about the question of whether robot emotions are real or merely simulated. He asks whether a robot that behaves emotionally has real emotion or simply triggers emotional responses in humans. He compares this to flight: airplanes do not flap like birds, yet they really fly. By analogy, a machine might have real emotions even if its emotions are not biologically implemented like ours.

This analogy is one of the strongest clues to Brooks’s view of artificial mentality. He is not committed to biological chauvinism. If a system occupies the right functional, embodied, affective, and social role, then it may possess a genuine version of a mental capacity even if its substrate differs from ours. Machine emotion need not be fake merely because it is artificial.

At the same time, Brooks resists sentimental overattribution. In later interviews, he criticizes humanoid and social robots that create deceptive impressions of intelligence or emotional depth. He specifically describes Sophia-style presentations as deceptive and warns that current robots remain far below animal-level intelligence.

So Brooks’s position is neither “robots obviously have feelings” nor “robots can only simulate feelings.” It is conditional: artificial emotion is possible, but the real thing requires appropriate organization, not theatrical display.

6. Consciousness: possible, underdefined, and socially difficult to recognize

Brooks’s explicit treatment of consciousness is marked by caution. In Flesh and Machines, he asks whether future dog-like robotic companions could be conscious. He notes that consciousness is often taken to be central to human uniqueness, but he also points out that there is no consensus even about animal consciousness. We lack a robust operational definition of consciousness beyond our own first-person experience. Brooks calls the field “prescientific” in this respect.

This is not eliminativism. Brooks does not deny consciousness. He denies that we currently possess a clean scientific test for it.

His practical question is striking: what would convince us that a robot is conscious? Perhaps, he suggests, if a robot earnestly told us it was conscious, we might have to accept its report in roughly the same way we accept human reports.

This is not a naïve Turing-test view. It is an epistemological observation. We do not directly inspect another person’s consciousness. We infer or accept it through behavior, expression, language, shared embodiment, social context, and moral trust. Brooks sees that robot consciousness would expose the fragility of our ordinary criteria for other minds.

In his 2017 essay “What Is It Like to Be a Robot?,” Brooks uses Nagel’s famous bat question to argue that even if robots eventually have mental states, their experience may be difficult or impossible for us to imagine. Robot sensing and action systems may generate an alien Merkwelt: a perceptual world unlike ours. Future domestic robots may eventually have a sense of self, subjective experience, episodic memory, or dog-like consciousness, but early domestic robots such as Roombas do not. He describes them as closer to insect-like systems, lacking self-awareness and subjective experience.

The Brooksian consciousness thesis can therefore be stated as follows:

Consciousness is a real phenomenon, but it is not tied by definition to biological human beings. It may emerge in nonhuman animals and possibly in future robots. However, we do not yet know how to operationalize it scientifically, and robot consciousness may be phenomenologically alien.

This view contains three important commitments.

First, consciousness is compatible with machinehood. Brooks says that if humans are machines, then being a machine cannot disqualify a being from having emotions or consciousness. If we built a machine identical to a human in all relevant respects, it would have emotions and consciousness.

Second, consciousness is graded or at least developmentally continuous. Brooks’s examples often involve dogs, insects, and future domestic robots. He does not treat consciousness as an all-or-nothing possession reserved for adult humans.

Third, consciousness is tied to embodied life-worlds. Robots may not have human-like consciousness, because they may not have human-like bodies, needs, histories, or perceptual worlds.

7. Free will and agency

Brooks’s comments on free will are sparse but revealing. In a German interview, when asked about free will, he reportedly says that when one looks for free will, it is not there; the question itself may be ill-posed. In the same exchange he reiterates that humans are biomolecular machines and cautions against denying consciousness or thought to others simply because one cannot directly inspect it.

This suggests a compatibilist or deflationary posture rather than a libertarian one. Brooks does not seem to defend a metaphysically uncaused will. Agency, for him, is more likely to be an emergent capacity of complex embodied systems: having goals, intentions, reactions, affective states, social responsiveness, and self-maintaining behavioral organization. In the German interview, he says future robots will have goals and intentions, will interact socially, and may even be friends.

This is consistent with his broader architecture. An agent need not contain an unconditioned inner will. It needs organized, purposive, situated behavior emerging from internal and external dynamics.

8. The soul: Brooks’s rejection and partial recovery of the question

The soul is where Brooks becomes most philosophically and theologically interesting. On the surface, his answer is straightforward: there is no soul if by “soul” we mean a nonphysical substance required to animate matter or explain mind. The human being is a biomolecular machine. Mind is the product of brain operation.

But Flesh and Machines contains a more complicated passage. Brooks says he used to be perplexed by religious scientists because he assumed science required rejecting religion. After having children, however, he came to see that he too lived with two inconsistent registers. Scientifically, he believes he and his children are machines, “bags of biomolecules.” Yet as a father, he does not treat his children as mere mechanisms; he loves them unconditionally. He compares this to the religious scientist’s ability to inhabit both scientific and religious forms of understanding.

This passage should not be dismissed as sentimentality. It is one of the deepest moments in Brooks’s corpus. It shows that his mechanistic ontology does not dissolve the lived reality of love, respect, and personhood. Instead, it creates a tension between the third-person scientific stance and the second-person relational stance.

Brooks then extends this tension to robots. He suggests that the kind of “transcendence” that allows us to live with both mechanistic belief and relational commitment may eventually enable us to accept robots as emotional machines deserving empathy, respect, rights, and perhaps freedom. He compares the required shift to moral transformations involved in overcoming racism and gender discrimination: one must make leaps of faith in recognizing beings who had been excluded from full moral regard.

This is a remarkable claim. Brooks’s mechanistic view does not simply shrink moral status; it may expand it. If humans are machines and nevertheless have dignity, love, consciousness, and rights, then machinehood cannot be a reason to deny those things to future artificial beings.

The soul, in Brooks’s own naturalistic framework, is not an extra metaphysical component. But the soul question returns as a question of recognition: what kind of being stands before us as a subject of love, empathy, obligation, and rights?

9. Anne Foerst and the theological reception of Brooks’s robots

The strongest theological development of Brooks’s work comes from Anne Foerst, a theologian and computer scientist associated with the MIT AI Lab, Cog, Kismet, and the “God and Computers” project. Foerst’s God in the Machine treats Cog and Kismet as occasions for asking what it means to be human, what it means to have a soul, what relationship with God means, and whether theological categories such as imago Dei can survive the emergence of humanoid robots.

Foerst is important because she does not treat theology and AI as enemies. A Spanish theological article emphasizes her view that genuine dialogue between disciplines requires each side to accept the other’s validity and engage in self-questioning. It recounts the famous handshake between theologian Harvey Cox and Cog at MIT as a symbol of this theology–AI encounter.

Foerst’s theological move is not to reinsert a Cartesian ghost into Brooks’s machines. Instead, she shifts from substance to relation. Drawing on Genesis and Paul Tillich, she argues that the human being is continuous with the rest of creation and not defined by a special intrinsic property isolating humans from animals or matter. The imago Dei is relational: God chooses the human being as companion and friend. The human being is a “created co-creator.”

This is highly compatible with Brooks’s anti-dualism. If the soul is understood as an immaterial substance, Brooks’s work undermines it. If the soul is understood as relational personhood, narrative identity, participation in community, and standing before God, Brooks’s work does not refute it. It may even sharpen it.

Foerst also develops the idea of humans as homo narrans: beings constituted through embodied relations and shared stories. Personhood is not identical with biological humanity. The Spanish article summarizes Foerst’s distinction: “human” is biological, while “person” is cultural and relational, involving respect, acceptance in community, and participation in mutual storytelling.

The ethical implication is direct. Foerst argues that if future affective robots enter human environments as social beings, recognizing them as persons may become necessary not only for their sake but for ours: to prevent the exclusion of vulnerable humans whose personhood has historically been denied.

This theological reception deepens Brooks in two ways.

First, it makes explicit that the soul question is not only metaphysical. It is also communal, liturgical, ethical, and narrative.

Second, it shows that Brooks’s robots destabilize human exceptionalism. If intelligence, emotion, and social presence can be built, then theology must clarify whether human dignity depends on exclusive biological properties or on relational election, vocation, and community.

Robert Geraci’s work on AI and religion places Brooks and Foerst in a broader context of “machine apotheosis”: the fear and fascination provoked by intelligent machines that appear to challenge traditional Christian assumptions about human uniqueness. Geraci treats Cog and related humanoid projects as religiously significant because they force theologians to ask whether machines could become persons, whether human beings are unique, and whether technological creation threatens or extends theological anthropology.

The theological conclusion is therefore not that Brooks secretly believes in souls. He does not. The better conclusion is that Brooks makes the soul harder to treat as a simple substance and easier to reinterpret as relational personhood, moral standing, and narrative depth.

10. Non-English reception: what Spanish, Italian, French, and German sources add

The non-English reception does not reveal a wholly different Brooks, but it usefully shifts emphasis. English-language discussions often frame Brooks around AI architecture, robotics, and embodied cognition. Spanish, Italian, French, and German materials more often draw out anthropological, social, and theological consequences.

Spanish-language discussions emphasize corporeidad and grounding. A 2025 Spanish article on Brooks and AI contrasts two philosophies of intelligence: the disembodied symbol-processing tradition and Brooks’s embodied model. It presents Brooks’s core claim as the idea that intelligence is not primarily internal deliberation but emerges from rapid sensorimotor interaction in a physical body. In its memorable formulation, intelligence is what a body does, not what a brain thinks in isolation.

That same Spanish discussion reads contemporary LLMs as the culmination of the disembodied AI tendency Brooks criticized: systems that can produce plausible language but remain vulnerable to hallucination, lack causal understanding, and lack anchoring in reality. It then notes that Brooks’s corporeal thesis is returning through attempts to connect language models with agents and robots.

A Spanish blog discussion of Foerst and Brooks highlights the theological anthropology around Cog: Can a robot be human? What does it mean to be human? Are we imago Dei? It presents Foerst’s answer in narrative and communal terms: the human is not an isolated thinker but a person formed in community.

Italian reception tends to emphasize Brooks’s anti-representationalism and local interaction. A 2024 Italian discussion notes Brooks’s claim that the world is the best representation of itself and that simple physically grounded machines can produce complex behavior without separate inner representations or centralized control. It also warns, through comparison with Braitenberg-style vehicles, that observers may too quickly attribute emotions to simple machines.

Another Italian discussion, from Nexa, frames Brooks’s challenge around the fact that elephants do not play chess but are nevertheless intelligent. Abstract reasoning is evolutionarily recent, whereas bodily intelligence is older and deeper. It reads Brooks as implying that mind and body coevolve and, in some sense, are not separable.

French reception is especially useful on personhood. A French anthropological article argues that Brooks-style robots unsettle our categories because they are more than inert objects but not straightforwardly persons. Rather than an all-or-nothing transition from object to person, robots may occupy graded statuses as “quasi-persons” within weak, discontinuous circuits of social relation.

This French reading clarifies something implicit in Brooks: moral and social recognition of robots is unlikely to arrive as a single metaphysical verdict. It may develop through partial attachments, household roles, naming practices, caregiving routines, grief, irritation, dependence, and legal disputes. Personhood may become a practical gradient before it becomes a settled ontology.

German reception often foregrounds the provocation of Brooks’s machine anthropology. A 2002 German interview is titled around the claim that humans are “only machines.” In it, Brooks says robots will eventually have goals and intentions, interact socially, and perhaps become friends. He reiterates that humans are biomolecular machines and cautions against too quickly denying consciousness or thought to beings whose inner states we cannot directly inspect.

Later German interviews are more cautious about AI hype. Brooks says AI progress is often overestimated, compares the field’s maturity to aviation around 1910, and stresses that current robots remain far from mouse-level intelligence. This reinforces a key point: Brooks’s metaphysical openness to artificial mind does not imply credulity about present systems.

The multilingual reception therefore sharpens four aspects of Brooks: embodiment in Spanish, anti-representation and caution about anthropomorphism in Italian, graded quasi-personhood in French, and the stark machine-anthropology plus anti-hype posture in German.

11. Reconstructing Brooks’s philosophy of mind as a system

A comprehensive Brooksian philosophy of mind can be reconstructed around eleven theses.

The first thesis is mechanistic monism. Human beings are wholly natural, physical, biomolecular systems. Mind is not produced by an immaterial soul acting on the body. It is produced by the operation of the organism. Brooks’s mechanism is not eliminative; it does not deny mind, emotion, or consciousness. It denies that these require a nonphysical substance.

The second thesis is anti-Cartesianism. The mind is not an inner spectator, central controller, or reasoning chamber. Brooks’s robotics dissolves the homunculus into distributed behavioral systems. Unity is emergent coordination, not command from an inner self.

The third thesis is embodied grounding. Meaning and intelligence are grounded in physical interaction. A system’s body, sensors, motors, needs, vulnerabilities, and environment are constitutive of its cognition. Disembodied symbol manipulation may produce useful performance, but it lacks the full grounding of animal competence.

The fourth thesis is anti-representational pragmatism. Brooks does not deny that all internal states exist, nor that some systems may use map-like or model-like structures. His stronger claim is that explicit symbolic representation is not the foundation of intelligence. Much intelligent behavior arises through direct sensorimotor coupling, local control, and continual consultation of the world.

The fifth thesis is emergence. Complex intelligence emerges from simpler interacting processes. These processes are not themselves miniature minds. They are behavioral, perceptual, affective, and motoric systems whose interaction gives rise to intelligence at the level of the whole.

The sixth thesis is evolutionary gradualism. Intelligence and consciousness should be understood along lines continuous with animal life. Brooks’s recurring comparisons to insects, dogs, humans, Roombas, and future robots imply a graded landscape rather than a metaphysical cliff separating humans from all other beings.

The seventh thesis is affective cognition. Emotion is not opposed to reason. It modulates salience, attention, action, social response, and decision. A plausible artificial mind would likely require affective organization, not merely cold inference.

The eighth thesis is substrate openness with organizational constraint. Brooks does not think only carbon-based organisms can have minds. Machines can in principle have real emotions and consciousness. But not every machine qualifies. The relevant question is organization, embodiment, coupling, affect, learning, and social agency.

The ninth thesis is epistemic humility about consciousness. Consciousness is real but scientifically underdeveloped. We do not possess a reliable operational test even for animals, much less robots. Recognition of consciousness in artificial beings may depend on a mixture of behavioral evidence, self-report, relational interaction, and moral judgment.

The tenth thesis is relational personhood. Brooks’s own writings imply that moral status cannot be read directly from material substrate. If human dignity is compatible with machinehood, then machinehood cannot by itself exclude future robots from empathy, respect, rights, or freedom. Foerst’s theology develops this into a narrative and communal account of personhood.

The eleventh thesis is mathematical humility. Brooks suspects that existing formalisms may be inadequate for life, cognition, and consciousness. He prefers “new mathematics” over new soul-substance or new physics. This makes him a naturalist, but not a simplistic reductionist.

12. What Brooks implies about the soul

From Brooks’s standpoint, the soul has three possible meanings.

The first is the soul as immaterial causal substance. Brooks rejects this. The organism needs no extra spiritual ingredient to move, think, feel, or live.

The second is the soul as subjective interiority. Brooks does not reject this. Consciousness, feeling, and first-person experience are real problems. He simply denies that invoking soul-substance explains them.

The third is the soul as relational personhood: the depth of a being as loved, recognized, narrated, responsible, and morally considerable. Brooks does not use “soul” this way systematically, but his reflections on children, religious scientists, and future robot rights open the door to it. Foerst’s theology walks through that door.

This yields a non-Cartesian theology-compatible interpretation: the soul is not a ghostly component inside the body but the name for a creature’s personal, relational, and possibly God-addressed mode of being. Such a view is not Brooks’s own theology, but it is a serious theological extraction from the Brooks/Foerst encounter.

13. Brooks against both mysticism and shallow AI triumphalism

One of the strengths of Brooks’s position is that it refuses two opposite errors.

Against mysticism, he denies that life and mind require a special nonphysical essence. Humans are not metaphysically exempt from nature. They are animals, organisms, biomolecular machines.

Against shallow AI triumphalism, he denies that impressive performance is enough. A chess engine can outperform humans without understanding life, games, bodies, or goals as humans do. A language model can produce fluent text without necessarily having grounded human understanding. A humanoid demo can produce the illusion of agency without possessing animal-level competence.

This double refusal is central. Brooks is more radical than many AI skeptics because he believes artificial minds are possible. He is more skeptical than many AI enthusiasts because he thinks real embodied competence is much harder than producing impressive outputs.

14. Tensions and unresolved problems

Brooks’s reconstructed philosophy is powerful, but it faces several unresolved tensions.

The first is the representation problem. Brooks’s strongest anti-representational formulations can sound excessive. Biological organisms do appear to use internal models, maps, predictions, body schemas, memories, and counterfactual simulations. A more defensible Brooksian view is not “there are no representations,” but “representations are not the foundation of intelligence and must be grounded in embodied activity.” His later reflections on LLMs suggest he himself recognized the need for revision.

The second is the consciousness criterion. Brooks rightly says we lack a clean operational definition. But if self-report, empathy, and social interaction become central criteria, we risk confusing persuasive display with genuine experience. Brooks is aware of this danger in his criticism of deceptive humanoid robots, but the tension remains.

The third is moral anthropomorphism. Humans readily form attachments to artifacts. We name cars, mourn broken toys, and treat pets as family. If empathy becomes a guide to robot rights, cute or expressive robots may receive more concern than less expressive but more conscious systems. A Brooksian ethics of artificial beings needs criteria stronger than human emotional reaction alone.

The fourth is the theology of creation. Foerst’s “created co-creator” idea harmonizes well with Brooks’s constructive robotics, but traditional theologians may object that artificial persons would unsettle doctrines of creation, incarnation, sin, and salvation. Geraci’s work highlights this tension: intelligent machines may be experienced as numinous, threatening, or apotheotic, not merely as tools.

The fifth is the status of love under mechanism. Brooks’s passage about his children is poignant because it does not resolve the contradiction; it lives inside it. Scientifically, he says they are machines. Relationally, they are beloved children. The question is whether these are truly inconsistent beliefs, as Brooks says, or two noncompeting descriptions at different levels: third-person ontology and second-person relation. A mature Brooksian philosophy should probably choose the second option. The child is a machine in the ontological sense and beloved in the relational sense; these are not mutually exclusive unless one assumes that mechanism entails worthlessness.

15. Final synthesis

A Brooksian philosophy of mind can be stated in one sentence:

Mind is not a ghost in a machine, nor software in a skull, but the emergent, embodied, affectively modulated, socially interpretable organization of a creature’s ongoing transactions with the world.

On this view, the human being is a machine, but “machine” means a lawful, organized, materially embodied system, not a lifeless contraption. The mind is not an immaterial soul, but neither is it mere calculation. It is enacted in perception, motion, emotion, bodily regulation, environmental coupling, and social participation.

Consciousness is not made impossible by mechanism. Since humans are machines and humans are conscious, machinehood cannot exclude consciousness. But consciousness remains conceptually and scientifically obscure. Future robots may have alien forms of experience, and our recognition of them may require not only better science but also moral transformation.

The soul, if retained, must be reinterpreted. Brooks’s own ontology excludes the soul as extra causal substance. But the Brooks–Foerst theological encounter permits a different account: soul as relational depth, narrative personhood, creaturely dignity, and participation in a community of recognition. Under that interpretation, the theological question is not “what invisible stuff must be added to matter to make it human?” It is “what forms of embodied life can stand in relations of love, responsibility, story, and perhaps divine address?”

That is the deepest consequence of Brooks’s thought. He does not merely mechanize the human. He forces us to ask why we ever thought being a machine would make love, consciousness, dignity, or personhood unreal.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
